column1: # Source: Z-Beam Laser Cleaning Research Lab
# Citation: Z-Beam (2025). Plaster Laser Cleaning Dataset. Retrieved from http://localhost:3000/materials/masonry/general/plaster-laser-cleaning

column9: Optimal average power for plaster surface cleaning without thermal damage
Near-infrared wavelength optimized for plaster absorption and minimal substrate damage
Beam spot diameter for balanced cleaning efficiency and resolution
Optimal repetition rate for efficient cleaning coverage and thermal management
Fluence level sufficient for contaminant removal while preserving plaster substrate
Nanosecond pulse duration for effective contaminant removal with controlled thermal penetration
Optimal scanning speed for uniform cleaning without excessive dwell time
Recommended number of passes for complete contaminant removal
Optimal beam overlap for uniform cleaning coverage and efficiency

Typical range for industrial laser cleaning of delicate materials like plaster to avoid thermal damage; starting at 100 W for light contamination removal. Max safe below 500 W to prevent substrate cracking due to plaster's low thermal conductivity (~0.5 W/m·K). Source: Manufacturer specs from IPG Photonics YLR series and studies on laser cleaning of gypsum-based plasters (e.g., J. Cult. Herit. 2018).

Effective range for 1064 nm cleaning on plaster; slower speeds (500-1000 mm/s) for heavy contamination, faster (2000-3000 mm/s) for light. Recommended 1500 mm/s balances efficiency and safety. Source: Industry standards from Laser Cleaning Europe guidelines and equipment manuals (e.g., Adapt Laser Systems).

Nanosecond regime preferred for cost-effective cleaning of plaster; picosecond (ps) or femtosecond reduces HAZ but less common at 1064 nm for industrial use. 20 ns minimizes heat accumulation on low-melting-point plaster (~150°C dehydration). Source: Laser physics research in Opt. Lasers Eng. (2015) on ablation of calcium sulfate materials.

Typical for pulsed 1064 nm lasers in cleaning; 30 kHz recommended for plaster to achieve fast coverage without excessive heat buildup, trading off speed vs. thermal load. Higher rates (>50 kHz) risk accumulation on low-conductivity substrates. Source: Academic literature, e.g., Appl. Surf. Sci. (2019) on stone/plaster cleaning parameters.

Fluence range for contaminant removal without ablating plaster; damage threshold ~1.5-2.0 J/cm² for gypsum due to reflectivity (~0.3 at 1064 nm) and low melting point. Recommended 0.8 J/cm² for safe surface preparation. Source: Ablation threshold studies in J. Appl. Phys. (2017) and historical artifact cleaning papers.

Beam diameter range for precision vs. area coverage; 300 μm recommended for plaster to focus energy on contaminants while sparing porous substrate. Smaller for fine details, larger for broad industrial prep. Source: Equipment specifications from Coherent and Trumpf laser cleaners.

Single pass for light contamination, multi-pass (2-5) for heavy layers on plaster to gradually remove without damage. Recommended 2 passes for uniform results in industrial settings. Source: Industry case studies, e.g., restoration projects in Constr. Build. Mater. (2020).

Ensures uniform coverage without gaps or overexposure; 50% recommended for plaster cleaning to optimize energy distribution and minimize uneven heating. Typical 30-80% based on scan patterns. Source: Process optimization studies in Lasers Manuf. Mater. Process. (2016).
